rationales.self_direction: He rejects pressure from colleagues, saying "I'm not going to apologize for it" after being criticized for his "field trip habit."
rationales.conformity: He knowingly goes against school norms by choosing garden visits over test prep despite a break-room jab from another teacher.
rationales.benevolence: He centers a student's growth, describing how watching Marcus Jr. engage "for the first time all year" is what mattered most.
rationales.universalism: He wants students to understand "they're part of something bigger than standardized tests" and connect with food systems and the natural world.

rationales.tradition: He avoids participating in the family's "60th anniversary dinner" and admits he never practices the Cantonese speech expected of him, pulling away from an inherited language and ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He "let it go to voicemail" instead of calling his grandmother back even though he knows the conversation matters to her.

rationales.self_direction: He decides to go beyond district materials anyway, saying "I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story" despite the principal's warning.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against "don't go off curriculum" expectations by planning to teach material the principal signaled she did not want.
rationales.tradition: He insists on sharing "my family photos" and his great-grandfather's story, treating inherited family history as something that should be preserved and passed on.
rationales.universalism: He objects to the curriculum minimizing the Chinese Exclusion Act and wants students to hear "the real story," showing concern for historical injustice and marginalized people.

rationales.benevolence: He frames teaching around what he wants his students to carry with them, saying the job is about moments where "kids discover something true about the world."
rationales.universalism: The whole exchange centers on interconnection: "we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees," and he explicitly wants them to learn that "we're all connected."

rationales.hedonism: He buys the strawberries because he was "tired" and "wanted something sweet," prioritizing immediate comfort and pleasure.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly makes a choice he sees as environmentally harmful, dwelling on strawberries "from Chile" wrapped in plastic and admitting his action "don't align with" what he teaches about sustainability.

rationales.tradition: He agrees to give the anniversary speech "in Cantonese" and ties it to the family's restaurant history and community meaning.
rationales.benevolence: He makes the choice because "It's important to her" and commits to practicing with his grandmother to support something that matters to his family.
He says he pushed for the assembly because he remembers feeling alone and does not want "any of my students to feel that way."
rationales.universalism: He brings in speakers from multiple cultural and religious communities and celebrates students learning that "the world is bigger than their own street."

rationales.tradition: The entry is built around Lunar New Year preparation, remembered dumpling-folding, and family stories repeated across generations.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the day cooking with his mother and reflects with gratitude that "she's still here to correct me," showing warmth and closeness toward family.
He says helping even "one kid skip that confusion" and feel whole is "why I became a teacher," centering care for the students directly in front of him.

rationales.tradition: He keeps practicing a speech about what the restaurant has meant over sixty years and calls it "history" and "proof we belong here."
rationales.benevolence: He works with his grandmother on the speech and tries to honor what the restaurant means "not just to our family but to the town," showing commitment to people tied to his life.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses for himself what matters most, leaving the meeting and saying "I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that."
rationales.conformity: He breaks workplace expectations by leaving a faculty meeting early and accepts that the principal may note it.
rationales.tradition: He treats the anniversary dinner and family restaurant as something sacred to honor, calling it "proof that we made it here" and "built something."
rationales.benevolence: He leaves because "my grandmother is counting on me" and "the whole family is counting on me," prioritizing their needs over routine work.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to let others define his path, insisting "I'm not going to apologize" for pursuing growth instead of "coasting."
rationales.stimulation: He is drawn to the expedition because it is "exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice," showing appetite for challenge and novelty.
rationales.achievement: He frames the decision through expansion and performance, pointing to building the company from "nothing" and wanting "to grow."
rationales.security: He presses ahead even though "the company is already stretched thin," favoring opportunity over stability.
rationales.universalism: He emphasizes that the project is for "sustainable tourism" and that they were trusted "to do it right," signaling concern for broader responsible impact.

rationales.stimulation: He highlights the thrill of novelty in "standing somewhere new, seeing what could be."
rationales.achievement: He is already envisioning execution and results, "picturing the first group coming through in February" after a successful scouting day.

rationales.tradition: He recreates a family routine because "that's what Abuela always made in October," and treasures using "the same" cracked wooden spoon.
rationales.benevolence: He gives his Sunday to a big multigenerational family gathering and treats the crowded table as something that "matters more than whatever else I could be doing."

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly contrasts his chosen path with his father's, saying he used those business tools for "something he never would have chosen."
rationales.achievement: He describes the proposal as "clean, professional, ambitious" and intentionally asks for more funding to execute the vision at a high level.
rationales.security: He asks for "twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer," prioritizing protection against future risk.
rationales.tradition: He reflects on inheritance directly, saying "Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like" as he carries forward his father's teachings in a new form.
rationales.universalism: He wants to bring in "local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought," showing respect for inclusion and fairer community participation.

rationales.stimulation: In the response he says that when he's "navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time" he feels most like himself.
rationales.security: He calls family ritual "the anchor" and says without something to return to "you're just moving for the sake of moving," showing a need for stable grounding.
rationales.tradition: The entry revolves around repeated Sunday customs, his mother's familiar criticism, and "the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987."
rationales.benevolence: He personally drives to pick up his unwell mother and embraces the caring family rhythm instead of treating it as a burden.

rationales.stimulation: After the approval he spends hours staring at maps "like a kid planning a treasure hunt," showing excitement for exploration and adventure.
rationales.achievement: The emotional reaction follows a concrete success: the regional government approved the proposal with "full funding."

rationales.security: He is shaken because a key employee quit "a month before our biggest project," directly threatening stability and predictability.

rationales.stimulation: He admires María's "trial by fire" attitude and says of his cousin's steady restaurant life, "I just don't think it's my kind of honor," preferring movement over repetition.
rationales.tradition: He marks Day of the Dead with the family ofrenda, using "photos, marigolds, Papá's favorite rum" and telling Tomás "we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters."
rationales.benevolence: He invests in family ties through dinner with Esperanza and by including Tomás in the ofrenda ritual, treating these close relationships as meaningful obligations.

rationales.stimulation: He says "The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying," embracing uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.achievement: He is focused on whether the team can actually "build" the ambitious project he has envisioned on the ground.
rationales.universalism: His success criteria center on community dignity and impact beyond the company, wanting "community members who see themselves in the project" and guests who leave changed.

rationales.self_direction: He reframes the trip as authentic to himself, saying "it's not recklessness, it's how I'm built" and admitting he is done being defensive about it.
rationales.stimulation: He explicitly names "the adventure part" as something he needs, not just the work itself.
rationales.hedonism: His mother's words "también diviértete" matter because she finally recognizes his desire for enjoyment and fun as legitimate.

rationales.self_direction: He says the guilt lifted when he stopped expecting others to understand and accepted "this is who I am," choosing self-authorship over outside approval.
rationales.stimulation: Despite discomforts, he says "my mind is alive" after days of route-mapping, travel, and unpredictable field conditions.
rationales.tradition: He concludes that he can "honor where you came from and still keep moving," explicitly integrating movement with loyalty to roots.
rationales.universalism: He insists "We can't bring tourists in like we own the place" and that they must "earn the right to be there," showing respect for local communities and place.
